MSRE Design and Operations Report, Part XI-A, Test Program for $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ Operation, by J.
The initial critical operation of the MSRE with $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ occurred on June 1, 1965.
The reactor was subsequently operated at various powers up to 8 Mw for sustained periods and accumulated a total of 9005 equivalent full-power hours with that fuel.
The reactor loop was drained on March 29, 1968 and preparations were started to replace the $^{235}\mathrm{U}-^{238}\mathrm{U}$ mixture in the fuel salt with $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
The test program that was conducted with the $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ is described in Reference 1.
The purpose of this memo is to outline the program that is to be followed with the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ loading.
Some useful correlations were developed during the $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ operation and it may be practical to use similar correlations to monitor reactor performance.
Of particular interest in the area of performance tests will be a special experiment to measure the effective neutron yield for $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ in a molten salt reactor neutron spectrum.
The $^{23}\mathrm{U}$ fuel mixture will probably be used for all the remaining operation of the MSRE.

In addition to providing a check on the calculational techniques used to predict the properties of the MSRE with $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ fuel, measurements of these basic properties will supply much of the data that is required for monitoring the subsequent behavior of the reactor.
However, a small heel of fuel salt (containing about $1.2\mathrm{kg}$ of the total U) will be left in a fuel drain tank when the salt is transferred to the fuel storage tank for processing.
This uranium will be mixed with the fuel carrier salt before the ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ critical experiment is started.

Most of the plutonium and many of the non-volatile fission products (notably samarium) that were produced in the ${}^{235}\mathrm{U}$ operation will remain in the salt for the ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ operation.
Thus, the ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ critical experiment will not be "clean" and corrections for the effects of these contaminants will have to be made when the results are evaluated.
Except for a practice addition of about $0.8\mathrm{kg}$ of ${}^{238}\mathrm{U},^*$ all of the uranium that is added in the critical experiment will have the isotopic composition listed in Table 1.
This uranium is available as the eutectic salt mixture LiF-UF4 (73 - 27 mole %) in special cans containing up to 7 kg of total U.
As in the initial ${}^{235}\mathrm{U}$ critical experiment, most of the uranium will be added to the fuel salt in a drain tank (FD-2).

Since the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ mixture is heavily contaminated with $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ and the last chemical purification of the uranium occurred some 4 years ago, the enriching salt contains substantial amounts of the daughter products of $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ decay.
2 (FD-2), as shown in Fig.

This equipment permits the transfer of single cans of enriching salt (containing no more than $7\mathrm{kg}$ of U) from the shielded transport cask into the drain tank under shielded, controlled-ventilation conditions.
The nuclear reactivity of a drain tank containing $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ fuel will be somewhat higher than the same drain tank containing the $^{235}\mathrm{U}-^{238}\mathrm{U}$ mixture.
The initial charging operation will require the addition of three 7-kg cans of uranium to FD-2.
The cans are designed to be remotely suspended in the gas space of FD-2 above the liquid carrier salt.
After three cans of enriching salt (21-kg U) have been added in this manner, the mixed fuel salt will be loaded into the reactor.
Since the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ mixture provides such an intense internal neutron source, count rates with the external source and no fuel are of little value in this critical experiment.
Thus two loadings of predetermined size (21-kg and 7-kg) are required before extrapolations can be made to establish the size of subsequent loadings.

Although there will be no change in the basic configuration of the reactor, the control rods in the MSRE will have about $30\%$ more reactivity worth with $^{23}U$ fuel than with the original loading.
The basic approach to be used for this work will be the same as that used for the original calibration. $^{2,3}$

Since uranium can only be added in increments of 98 grams, each capsule will increase the fuel reactivity by about $0.12\%$ $\delta \mathrm{k} / \mathrm{k}$ , so about 24 capsules will be needed to produce full insertion of one rod.
This expression will then be used (as it was during the $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ operation) by the on-line computer to calculate control-rod poisoning from the positions of the three rods.

As excess $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ is added to the loop to bring the concentration to the operating value and calibrate the control rods, data will be collected to evaluate the reactivity effect of the excess uranium.
The reactor control systems (flux, temperature, and load) were tested under a variety of conditions with $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ fuel to demonstrate their adequacy.
A similar series of tests will be performed with the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ fuel.
However, this time the main emphasis will be on the reactor servo controller, flux servo at low power and temperature servo at powers above 1 Mw.

Calculations, analog-simulator studies, and measurements on the MSRE indicated that, at most, the high-frequency gain of the flux servo may have to be adjusted slightly for satisfactory performance with $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
(Reference 5) If such changes are made, the pertinent tests will be repeated to demonstrate the adequacy of the final system.
The dynamic behavior of the MSRE with $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ fuel was the subject of extensive theoretical and experimental investigation.
A comparable theoretical analysis of the dynamics with $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ has been performed and the results indicate that the reactor will be inherently stable at all powers.
As with the $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ operation, various dynamic tests will be performed at zero power and during the approach to full power.

However, since a detailed understanding of the xenon behavior is important to the breeder programs, an attempt will be made during the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ operation to modify the mathematical model to incorporate temperature and pressure effects.
In the course of operating the MSRE with $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ , a large amount of data was collected on the spectral density of the inherent neutron-level fluctuations in the reactor.

section: Measurement of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ Capture to Fission Ratio
text: An important factor in the breeding performance of molten salt reactors is the ratio of parasitic neutron captures to fissions ("alpha") in $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ in the reactor neutron spectrum.

section: Measurement of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ Capture to Fission Ratio
text: Since the neutron spectrum in the MSRE with $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ fuel is very similar to that in the proposed breeders, a precise measurement of "alpha" will be made during the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ operation.

section: Measurement of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ Capture to Fission Ratio
text: The measurement consists of precise uranium isotopic assays before and after substantial $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ burnup.

section: Measurement of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ Capture to Fission Ratio
text: The value of "alpha" is derived from the buildup of $^{234}\mathrm{U}$ relative to the depletion of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .

section: Surveillance of Corrosion and Salt Contamination
text: In the first three years of reactor operation, the chromium in the fuel salt increased from $\sim 38$ ppm to $\sim 80$ ppm.

section: Surveillance of Corrosion and Salt Contamination
text: If this is interpreted in terms of uniform attack on the fuel loop the increase represents leaching from less than 0.3 mil of metal.

section: Surveillance of Corrosion and Salt Contamination
text: The processing of the fuel salt to remove the $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ will require the establishment of a new baseline of chromium concentration.

section: Surveillance of Corrosion and Salt Contamination
text: The resultant chromium concentration will be measured in samples taken at the start of the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ operation.

section: Surveillance of Corrosion and Salt Contamination
text: The oxide tolerance of the MSRE fuel mixture is $\sim 700$ parts per million but observed concentrations have been around $50 - 60~\mathrm{ppm}$ .
